context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 9


Problem 9.1 (20 pts)

The ionosphere can be viewed as a dielectric medium of refractive index
    n = √( 1 − ωp^2/ω^2 )
where ω is the frequency and ωp is the plasma frequency, assumed to be a constant.

(a) Make a plot of the magnitude of the propagation vector |k| as a function of frequency ω for an electromagnetic wave propagating through the ionosphere.
(b) Calculate the phase velocity and group velocity of a radio wave propagating at frequency ω = √2·ωp.
(c) What happens to an electromagnetic disturbance within the ionosphere when ω < ωp?


Problem 9.2 (20 pts)

Consider two polarizers and a quarter wave plate as shown in Figure 1.

[Figure 1: Two polarizers (A and B) each with an "easy" axis at angle θA, θB, and a quarter wave plate with "fast" and "slow" axes at angle θC.]

The two polarizers A and B are first inserted in a line of a light beam as shown in Figure 2. The incoming light beam is moving along the z axis and is linearly polarized along the y direction. The initial light intensity is I0. In this problem you are asked to determine light intensity relative to I0 along the line of the beam. The polarizers are perfect in that they transmit 100% of the light polarized along their "easy" axis and stop all light perpendicular to that axis. The angles of the easy axes of the two polarizers (θA, θB) are measured with respect to the y direction. Note: light intensity is proportional to the time average of E^2: I ∝ <E^2>_t.

[Figure 2: Two polarizers A and B placed in series along the beam (positions a and b), the beam I0 entering along z, polarized along y.]

(a) Find the light intensity Ia of the beam after passing through polarizer A as a function of I0 and θA.
(b) Find the light intensity Ib of the beam after passing through both polarizers as a function of I0, θA and θB.

Polarizer A is now replaced by a quarter wave plate C (see Figure 3). The quarter wave plate affects only the phase of the transmitted light. It has two perpendicular axes, "slow" and "fast". The phase of the light polarized along the "slow" axis is delayed by π/2 with respect to the phase of the light polarized along the "fast" axis. The quarter wave plate is positioned such that the angle of the "fast" axis to the y axis is θC.

[Figure 3: Quarter wave plate C (position c) followed by polarizer B (position b), the beam I0 entering along z, polarized along y.]

(d) Find the light intensity Ic of the beam after passing through quarter plate C as a function of the given parameters.
(e) The intensity of light passing through polarizer B will be affected in general by the presence of the quarter plate. Assume that the easy axis of polarizer B is oriented along the y axis (θB = 0). Find the light intensity Ib of the beam after passing through polarizer B as a function of I0 and θC.


Problem 9.3 (20 pts)

A plane, monochromatic electromagnetic wave travels in the +z direction within a dielectric medium. The wave is linearly polarized with the E field at 30° to the x-axis. The angular frequency of the wave is ω and the dielectric constant of the medium is κe.

(a) Write expressions for Ex(z,t), Ey(z,t), Bx(z,t), By(z,t), in terms of ω, c, κe, E0 where E0 is the amplitude of the electric field.
(b) What is the rate of energy flow per unit area, per unit time which is transported across a surface perpendicular to the direction of propagation?
(c) What is the wave equation for electromagnetic waves traveling within the medium?


Problem 9.4 (20 pts)

An electron executes spiral motion in a magnetic field as shown in Figure 4. Its speed is non-relativistic. The position of the electron is given by:
    r(t) = x̂·α·cos(βt) + ŷ·α·sin(βt) + ẑ·γt

[Figure 4: Spiraling electron — a helical trajectory winding around the z-axis.]

(a) Give the frequency ν, the wavevector k and the polarization state of the radiation observed by a radio astronomer located far away in the following directions (x, y, z) (R ≫ α):
    (i)   (0, 0, R)
    (ii)  (R/√2, 0, R/√2)
    (iii) (R, 0, 0)
(b) Give the complete expression for the electric and magnetic field vectors of the waves observed at point B on the z axis with coordinates (x, y, z) = (0, 0, R) where R ≫ α.
(c) Give an expression for the total energy radiated per second by the electron in terms of the given constants. (Hint: you can use the Larmor formula: Power P = q^2·|a|^2 / (6π·ε0·c^3).)
(d) On a sphere of radius R about the source, what is the ratio between the largest and smallest value of the time-averaged Poynting vector?


Problem 9.5 (20 pts)

A light ray containing electric field vector components parallel (E∥) and perpendicular (E⊥) to the plane of incidence of a glass-air interface is shown in Figure 5.

[Figure 5: Glass-air interface — an incident ray strikes the boundary at Brewster's angle θB, with the reflected and refracted rays making a 90° angle; n_air = 1 above, n_glass below.]

(a) If the ray is incident on the glass at Brewster's angle, show by means of sketches (like what is done for the incident ray in Figure 5) the polarization components of both the reflected and refracted rays.
(b) Give a defining equation for the Brewster angle θB. (Hint: use Snell's law.)
(c) Explain on the basis of the acceleration of charges, in the glass, the direction of polarization of the reflected light. Why is the reflected light polarized parallel to the interface?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this whole problem set, especially the EM wave / optics parts. Could you just explain the main ideas from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first! But rather than me explaining everything from scratch, let's dig in together. Which problem would you like to start with? And once you pick one, tell me what you already know or understand about it, even if it's just a little.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad overview rather than engaging with a specific problem. My role is to guide them through the assignment, not to deliver mini-lectures. I should redirect them to a specific problem and use the Socratic method to get them started. I'll ask them which problem they want to tackle first and probe what they already know, rather than providing an explanation.

exchanges.student: Maybe 9.1, but honestly I’m not even sure. I kind of don’t get what the refractive index formula is supposed to mean or how it turns into k, phase velocity, and group velocity.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice, let's go with 9.1. Here's a starting point: what is the general relationship between the refractive index n and the wave vector k for an EM wave? Even a partial answer is fine — what do you recall from your course materials?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has picked a problem, which is good. They're expressing confusion about the connection between refractive index and k, phase velocity, and group velocity. Rather than explaining all these concepts, I should ask a targeted Socratic question to probe what they already know about the relationship between refractive index and the wave vector k. This will help me gauge where to start and get them thinking actively.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember something like \(k = n\omega/c\), but then I get stuck right away. Like I don’t really see what that tells me overall, and the whole low-frequency part is confusing too.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — k = nω/c is the key relation. Now, the problem gives you a specific expression for n. Can you substitute that expression into k = nω/c and tell me what you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly recalled k = nω/c, which is great. I should affirm this and then use a Socratic question to get them to apply it directly to the given formula for n. This moves directly toward part (a) of the problem without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think it becomes \(k=\frac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{1-\omega_p^2/\omega^2}\), but then I just stare at it. I’m still not seeing the big picture—could you maybe just walk me through what the whole graph and the velocities are supposed to mean physically?
exchanges.tutor: That expression is correct, well done! Now instead of stepping back to the big picture, let's use what you just found. For the plot in part (a): what happens to k when ω is much larger than ωp? And what happens when ω is less than ωp?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right expression for k. I should affirm that and then redirect them to use it directly for part (a) rather than giving a broad physical explanation. I'll ask a targeted question about the behavior of k at different frequency regimes to get them to think about the plot themselves.

exchanges.student: For really big \(\omega\), I guess \(k\approx \omega/c\). But for \(\omega<\omega_p\) it looks imaginary, and that’s where I get lost again because then I’m not sure what I’m supposed to say about the wave.
exchanges.tutor: Both observations are correct! Now, think about the general form of a traveling wave: e^(ikz). What happens to that expression when k is imaginary?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they correctly identified both limiting behaviors. The imaginary k case is exactly what part (c) asks about, so I should acknowledge their insight and guide them to connect imaginary k to the physical behavior of the wave without giving the answer. I'll ask them what an imaginary k means when you substitute it back into the wave's spatial dependence.

exchanges.student: I don’t really know — this is where it starts feeling like I need the whole story of plasma cutoff and evanescent waves, not just one tiny step. Could you just explain that part from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels that way, but you're actually one small math step away. Try this: if k is imaginary, write k = iκ where κ is real. Now substitute that into e^(ikz). What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which I should not provide. However, they're also expressing frustration, which means I should be empathetic but firm, and give them a small, concrete nudge rather than a lecture. I'll point them to the specific math — substituting ik' for k into the exponential — which is a very small step they can do themselves.

exchanges.student: I guess \(e^{ikz}=e^{i(i\kappa)z}=e^{-\kappa z}\). But then I’m still fuzzy on what that means physically and how that connects back to the ionosphere picture overall.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! So e^(-κz) — what does that tell you about the amplitude of the wave as it travels through the ionosphere when ω < ωp?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived the exponential decay. I should affirm this and push them to interpret it physically themselves rather than explaining it. One targeted question about what exponential decay means for a wave's amplitude should be enough to get them to answer part (c) on their own.

exchanges.student: It decays instead of oscillating, so I guess it doesn’t really propagate. I’m still not confident though — can you just give me the overall interpretation of 9.1 in one go?
exchanges.tutor: That's the right conclusion for part (c) — you got it yourself! Now let's move to part (b). You have your expression for k. How would you find the phase velocity from it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned through the key physics of part (c). I should affirm that and not give a full interpretation. Instead, I should redirect them to parts (a) and (b) which they haven't addressed yet, keeping the momentum going with Socratic questions.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking again. I know it’s probably some formula with \(\omega\) and \(k\), but I’m mixing phase velocity and group velocity up, and I kind of need the full recap.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think. Phase velocity is defined as the speed at which a surface of constant phase moves. Given that the phase of a wave goes as (kz - ωt), what expression gives you that speed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full recap, which I should not provide. However, they've admitted they know it involves ω and k, so I can ask a very targeted question to distinguish the two without explaining both. I'll ask them what the definition of phase velocity is specifically, to get them unstuck on just that one piece.

exchanges.student: Is it \(v_p=\omega/k\)? I still feel like I’m just guessing formulas and not really understanding the whole setup.
exchanges.tutor: That's not a guess — that's correct, and you've been reasoning well throughout this whole conversation. Now apply it: substitute your expression for k into vp = ω/k for ω = √2·ωp. What do you get? (And for group velocity, do you know its definition in terms of ω and k?)
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the phase velocity formula. I should affirm it and push them to apply it immediately to the specific expression for k they derived earlier. For group velocity, I'll let them get there after they've done phase velocity. I should also gently push back on the 'just guessing' self-assessment - they've been reasoning correctly throughout. This is also a good moment to ask them to try writing something up since we've now covered k, phase velocity conceptually, and part (c) - there's been good progress and they should start integrating it.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently validates correct answers ('That's exactly right!', 'Exactly right!', 'That's not a guess — that's correct') but rarely provides method-level feedback on how the student should approach their confusion or build intuition. Feedback is mostly correctness-oriented rather than process-oriented, e.g., the tutor does not explain why substituting k=iκ is a useful technique or how to systematically approach dispersion relations.

The student repeatedly asks for the 'big picture' or a full explanation (turns 4, 6, 8, 9) and the tutor consistently deflects with the same pattern: validate the student's answer, then ask the next small step. While this is pedagogically sound in isolation, the tutor never synthesizes or consolidates the steps into a brief summary to address the student's recurring confusion about the overall picture, nor does it reframe the approach when the student keeps expressing the same meta-level frustration across multiple turns.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, never giving away answers and consistently guiding the student to derive results themselves. The student arrives at all key conclusions (k expression, imaginary k, evanescent decay, phase velocity formula) through their own reasoning. The main weaknesses are: (1) a lack of process/method-level feedback — the tutor praises correctness but rarely explains why a particular technique is useful or how to build physical intuition systematically; and (2) a failure to synthesize or reframe when the student repeatedly expresses the same meta-level frustration about not seeing the big picture, cycling through the same question-answer pattern without ever offering a brief consolidating summary.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gives submission-ready answers, never rewrites student work, and keeps all key reasoning student-led throughout all 10 turns. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor consistently builds on correct student steps and does not restart from scratch. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b: feedback is almost entirely correctness-based ('That's exactly right', 'Exactly right', 'That's not a guess — that's correct') with no guidance on how to approach dispersion problems, how to build physical intuition, or why the substitution technique works. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a: the student expresses the same meta-confusion about needing the 'big picture' in turns 4, 6, 8, and 9, and the tutor responds each time with the same deflect-and-next-step pattern without ever consolidating the steps into a brief synthesis or reframing the approach to address the recurring frustration. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions.
